grammar.title: Imperative ‘manténganse’
grammar.detail: ustedes-imperative of reflexive mantenerse. The reflexive pronoun attaches; the resulting form is stressed on the antepenultimate syllable, so it takes a written accent.

grammar.title: Relative clause ‘a quienes amo’
grammar.detail: ‘quienes’ refers to people only (never things). Compare: ‘a quienes amo’ (whom I love), ‘que amo’ (also possible, more neutral).

expression.note: ‘my joy and my crown’ — affectionate metaphors. ‘Corona’ here is the victor’s wreath, not a royal crown. The Philippians are Paul’s trophy of grace.

vocab.meaning: to come to an agreement, to reconcile

grammar.title: rogar + a alguien + que + subjunctive
grammar.detail: Verbs of asking trigger subjunctive: ‘ruego a Evodia… que se pongan de acuerdo’. ‘pongan’ = present subjunctive of poner (here reflexive: ponerse).

grammar.title: Plural verb for two singular subjects
grammar.detail: Evodia + Síntique → ‘se pongan’ (plural). Even though each was addressed individually, Spanish makes them grammatical co-subjects.

expression.phrase: que se pongan de acuerdo en el Señor
expression.note: Two named women in conflict — Paul names them publicly. ‘en el Señor’ qualifies the agreement: not compromise, but reconciliation grounded in Christ.

grammar.title: Direct address ‘a ti’ + ‘te pido’
grammar.detail: Spanish often doubles the indirect/direct object with a pronoun for clarity or emphasis: ‘a ti… te pido’ = ‘you — I’m asking you’.

grammar.title: Present perfect ‘han luchado’
grammar.detail: Describes ongoing-and-still-relevant past struggle: ‘who have fought (and the fight matters now) alongside me’.

expression.note: An OT and apocalyptic image (Ex 32, Rev 21) — God’s register of his people. Paul places these unnamed co-workers in heavenly company.
